nativeRange.habitat: Hybrid/cultivar of horticultural origin; precise wild parentage is not formally documented but hobby literature commonly attributes the lineage to the Cryptocoryne wendtii complex. The Cryptocoryne genus is naturally distributed across tropical India, Southeast Asia, and New Guinea, typically in slow-flowing streams, rivers, and seasonally inundated forest pools.

decorPreferences: iron-rich root tabs near the root zone
high light to bring out intense pink/coral coloration
stable, soft, slightly acidic water to minimize crypt melt

avoidWith: Silver Dollars and other plant-eating fish

summary: A rare specialty Cryptocoryne cultivar prized for its ruffled leaves that emerge blood-red and mature to vibrant pink and coral tones. Demanding light, iron, and stable soft water for full color expression.
careNotes: Cryptocoryne 'Pink Flamingo' is a premium specialty cultivar in the planted-tank hobby, distinguished by ruffled leaves that emerge blood-red and gradually lighten to vivid pink and coral tones — a coloration unusual in the otherwise green/brown/bronze Cryptocoryne genus. Its precise taxonomic origin is not formally documented; it is most commonly described as a hybrid or designer cultivar associated with the Cryptocoryne wendtii lineage, and Buce Plant attributes the origin to Borneo (this regional attribution is treated cautiously — Wikipedia documents the broader genus as Indian/Southeast Asian/New Guinean, and Buce Plant uses the same blanket origin for many Crypts). Mature plants form compact rosettes on the smaller end of the Cryptocoryne range — typically 5-15 cm tall — making this a midground (sometimes foreground) specimen rather than a large background rosette. To bring out and hold the signature pink coloration, the prevailing hobby advice is HIGH lighting (low/medium light tends to produce washed-out, greener leaves; Buce Plant explicitly states color intensity scales with light and CO2), supplemental CO2 (Buce Plant: 'not necessary but recommended' — strongly advised for color intensity and growth rate), iron-rich fertilization (iron-dosed root tabs and water-column micronutrients are widely cited as critical for pink pigment expression), and soft, slightly acidic water (Buce Plant: pH 6-7). Like other Cryptocorynes, this is a HEAVY ROOT FEEDER — nutrient uptake happens primarily through the root zone, so a nutrient-rich substrate (aqua soil) and regular root tabs are strongly recommended. Plant by burying roots in substrate up to the crown (where leaves emerge from the rhizome); do NOT bury the crown itself. CRYPT MELT WARNING: Pink Flamingo is reported to be more sensitive to crypt melt than common Crypts like C. wendtii — newly planted specimens or those exposed to a sudden parameter shift frequently shed most or all of their leaves and may take 4-8 weeks to recover (Wikipedia notes ~30 days for genus-wide re-establishment). Stable parameters and avoiding relocation once planted are essential — Buce Plant emphasizes 'Cryptocoryne prefer not to be moved once planted. If possible, do not move them!' Cross-source confirmation was limited: Buce Plant (primary) confirmed pH 6-7, low-to-medium light tolerated but pink color requires more, CO2 'not necessary but recommended', moderate growth rate, easy difficulty once established, propagation by rhizome cutting, and Borneo origin. Wikipedia (genus-level secondary) confirmed Araceae/Alismatales taxonomy, broad SE Asian native range, ~12-33 °C tolerance for the genus, and the crypt-melt phenomenon. The Aquarium Co-Op product page returned 404 (likely delisted), Modern Aquarium returned 404, and Tropica's page rendered an empty template. Lower-stakes fields (temperature 22-28 °C, gH 2-15, kH 1-10, tank size, decor preferences, compatibility lists) are biologically reasonable for a wendtii-lineage cultivar but were not directly confirmed for the Pink Flamingo variety on a per-cultivar product page. Set to needs_review because Pink-Flamingo-specific cross-verification of high-stakes fields rested on a single product source (Buce Plant), with Wikipedia confirming only at the genus level.
breedingNotes: Propagated primarily by rhizome cutting/division — divide a healthy rhizome into sections, each retaining leaves and roots, and replant. Buce Plant explicitly notes 'cut by rhizome' as the propagation method. Runner (stolon) production typical of other Cryptocorynes is reportedly slower for this cultivar than for vigorous species like C. wendtii. Seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium and would require emersed flowering.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary product page. Confirmed pH 6-7, low-to-medium light tolerated (pink color requires more), CO2 'not necessary but recommended', moderate growth rate, midground placement, rhizome-cut propagation, easy-once-established difficulty, and Borneo origin attribution. Page is thin on temperature, gH/kH, adult size, and lifespan.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Cryptocoryne genus)
additional.notes: Genus-level cross-check: confirmed Araceae family, Alismatales order, native range across tropical India/Southeast Asia/New Guinea (does not corroborate Buce Plant's Borneo-specific attribution), tolerance temperature 12-33 °C, slightly alkaline to neutral pH for the genus, and the crypt-melt phenomenon (~30 days to re-establish). Wikipedia does not document Pink Flamingo specifically.
